text: The video shows a well-healed scar in the patient's right iliac fossa from a previous kidney transplantation. A single port laparoscopic method is marked inferior and to the right of the umbilicus, which will serve as the operative access site and the location for the end ileostomy.

The extraperitoneal transplanted kidney is visible in the right iliac fossa, with the transplanted ureter extending into the pelvis. The appendix is identified, and the small intestine is repositioned from the pelvis by tilting the patient head down and left side up, enhancing exposure of the distal sigmoid colon and proximal rectum. Dissection begins at the rectal sigmoid, with distal sigmoid staple transection proceeding proximally in a close pericolic plane.
The transplanted ureter and renal artery are clearly visible traveling into the pelvis.

The surgeon begins the dissection of the distal sigmoid colon from its mesocolon using a Medtronic ligature device, achieving precise hemostatic control. A window is created through the mesocolon, preparing for a stapled distal transection with an ENDO-GIA-SIXTY device.

The surgeon views the fully dissected distal colon, preparing to perform a transection using a stapler inserted through a glove port. The stapler is carefully positioned to ensure accurate placement, avoiding any structures of the pelvic side wall during the firing process.

The surgeon detaches the sigmoid colon from the rectum and begins dissecting the sigmoid mesocolon from the sigmoid colon, extending proximally. The stepwise dissection is visible, with careful traction applied by the assisting instrument providing counterpoint to the ligature device operated by the surgeon.

The surgeon moves the colon medially, revealing the left ureter along the pelvic wall at the bottom of the screen. The dissection remains in a close pericolic plane to avoid the retroperitoneal ureter and preserve the gonadal vessels. A bleeding mesocolic vessel is coagulated using a ligature device.

The surgeon approaches the descending colon, dissecting its lateral attachments using a lateral approach. A distracting instrument is frequently reapplied to maintain appropriate tension on the tissue being dissected.

The surgeon continues dissecting the descending colon, tilting the patient into a head-up, left-side-up position to facilitate bringing the splenic flexure into view. Both medial and lateral dissection techniques are employed, and the omentum is removed from its anterior and posterior attachments to the transverse colon.

The surgeon mobilizes the transverse colon by dissecting it from the omentum and its mesocolon, entering the lesser sac while carefully protecting the stomach and its vessels. The middle colic arteries are visible in this quadrant, requiring secure sealing to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon mobilizes the transverse colon along its distal two-thirds and proceeds to dissect the right colon, focusing on the cecum and ascending colon. The cecum, ileum, and appendix are elevated to facilitate dissection of the associated mesentery, revealing a clear plane between the right colon and the kidney transplant. The cecum and terminal ileum are freed, and dissection continues cephaladilaterally towards the hepatic flexure in a stepwise manner.
The surgeon dissects the mesocolon of the right colon, moving medially while ensuring the duodenum is visible and preserved.

The surgeon locates and removes the distal end of the colon through the single port access site. An extracorporeal separation of the ileum from the cecum is performed, and the ileum is returned to the abdominal cavity. Relaparoscopy is conducted to check for hemostasis, wash out any pooled fluid or blood, and verify the orientation of the distal ileum for end ileostomy formation.
